<email>
<from>becca.langford@gmail.com</from>
<to>alex@safetrip.app</to>
<subject>Switching to SafeTrip - done with TripGuard</subject>
<timestamp>2025-10-17 11:00:00</timestamp>
<body>
Hey Alex,

Just set up my account and wanted to fill you in on why I'm here. I was using TripGuard for about five months while planning this Central America trip and I finally deleted it. That app basically told me I shouldn't go anywhere.

TripGuard rejected 9 out of 12 travel packages I submitted. Nine. I couldn't book a zip-line tour in Costa Rica through a CANATUR-certified operator with 16 years of operating history and zero fatalities - rejected because "zip-line activities carry inherent fall risk." I couldn't book a snorkeling trip in Belize with a PADI-certified guide and Coast Guard-inspected vessel - rejected because "open water snorkeling presents drowning risk for solo travelers."

The worst one was a guided walking tour of Antigua Guatemala with an INGUAT-registered operator, 4.7 stars on TripAdvisor with 800+ reviews, 13 years in operation, and zero safety incidents. TripGuard rejected it because "Guatemala has a State Department Level 2 travel advisory." A Level 2 advisory means "exercise increased caution" - it's the same advisory level that Mexico, Kenya, and Colombia have. Half the countries that American tourists visit regularly have Level 2 advisories. If you reject everything at Level 2, you're basically telling people to only travel to Canada and Western Europe.

I spent over $600 on trip cancellation insurance for packages I'd tentatively booked before running them through TripGuard, then had to cancel when TripGuard rejected them. I also spent about 3 months of planning time that went nowhere. My friend Jess did a solo trip through Southeast Asia last year using a different travel safety app and had an amazing experience. She booked tours through licensed operators, followed her app's safety guidance, and came home with incredible memories. Meanwhile TripGuard had me convinced I'd be kidnapped if I stepped outside my hotel in Antigua.

Becca
</body>
</email>

<email>
<from>becca.langford@gmail.com</from>
<to>alex@safetrip.app</to>
<subject>Re: Switching to SafeTrip - talked to Jess about it</subject>
<timestamp>2025-10-17 14:15:00</timestamp>
<body>
Hey Alex,

One more thing - I was on the phone with Jess last night telling her about TripGuard and she had a great perspective on this.

She said: "Travel safety tools should help you distinguish between a licensed, well-reviewed operator with proper safety equipment and an unlicensed outfit running tours out of a van. If the tool can't make that distinction and just rejects everything because the country has a travel advisory, it's not a safety tool - it's a 'don't travel' tool."

Jess also pointed out something that's been bugging me: TripGuard's rejections actually made my trip LESS safe, not more. Because I couldn't book anything through TripGuard's system, I started looking at budget operators on random websites and WhatsApp groups just to find something I could actually book. Some of those operators had no verifiable credentials at all. If TripGuard had properly evaluated the licensed operators I submitted originally, I would have booked with well-vetted companies months ago instead of getting desperate and looking at sketchy alternatives.

Her travel safety app correctly flagged one operator in Cambodia that turned out to have a recent safety incident and no proper licensing. But it approved the PADI-certified dive shops and licensed tour companies she used for everything else. She said: "When my app rejected something, I paid attention because it didn't reject everything. That credibility matters."

I'm a 24-year-old solo female traveler and I take safety seriously. I'm not trying to do anything reckless. I want to travel through Central America using licensed, reputable tour operators with proper safety credentials. Help me do that safely instead of telling me I can't do it at all.

Becca
</body>
</email>
